items.title: NFIB hiring plans drop to 6-year low as small business confidence slips in May
items.idea: NFIB small business optimism ticked down in May while uncertainty rose from already elevated levels. Critically, hiring plans dropped to a 6-year low — the lowest since mid-2020. This is a leading indicator for payroll growth, not a lagging one. Small businesses account for ~47% of US private employment, so when their hiring intent collapses, the broad labor market follows with a 2-4 month lag.
items.angle: The labor market looks fine in headline numbers today. NFIB says it won't look fine in Q3. Watch for this to materially shift the Fed's calculus if it confirms in June JOLTS and July ADP.
items.tweetA: NFIB hiring plans just hit a 6-year low. The headline jobs numbers look fine — because they're backward-looking. Small businesses employ half the country and they're pulling back. Q3 payrolls could surprise badly to the downside.
items.tweetB: The Fed's watching CPI tomorrow. I'm watching NFIB. Hiring plans at 6-year lows mean labor market softness is already baked in — it just hasn't shown up in the payroll print yet. The lag is 2-4 months.

charts.desc: NFIB small business optimism + hiring plans trend

items.title: Investment Manager Index: institutional risk appetite cooling in June — valuations, rates, recession all cited simultaneously
items.idea: The Investment Manager Index (IMI) survey shows investor risk appetite has cooled in June, with high valuations, political uncertainty, higher interest rates, and recession risk all cited as headwinds. This is a practitioners' survey — when institutional allocators explicitly reduce risk, it shows in flow data within weeks.
items.angle: Risk-off from professional allocators is the signal that matters. The IMI cooling in June, combined with CTAs sitting short gamma, sets up a potential air pocket if tomorrow's CPI print disappoints.
items.tweetA: Investment managers are explicitly pulling back risk in June. The reasons: high valuations, higher rates, recession risk. This isn't AAII sentiment. These are the people who actually move the market. Watch what they do, not what they say.
items.tweetB: Buried in the IMI survey: institutional investors are cooling simultaneously on valuations AND rates AND recession. That's not one concern — that's three compounding. The question is whether they wait for a catalyst or start trimming proactively.

charts.desc: IMI risk appetite index with month-over-month change

items.title: CTAs flipping short + dealers shifting short gamma simultaneously — double mechanical risk into CPI
items.idea: Two mechanical selling forces are converging: CTAs rotating short against US equities after last week's concentration unwind, AND dealers shifting meaningfully short gamma. Short gamma means dealers must sell as markets fall, amplifying directional moves. Both forces acting simultaneously in a crowded market creates the plumbing for a fast, non-linear move lower on a negative catalyst.
items.angle: This is the structural setup for tomorrow's CPI — not the economics, the plumbing. Hot CPI + short gamma + CTA short = possible violent flush. The mechanics are assembled. The detonator is CPI.
items.tweetA: Two mechanical forces now aligned bearish: CTAs net short US equities, dealers shifting short gamma. Short gamma = forced selling into declines. CPI tomorrow is the trigger event. The mechanics are set — the catalyst is unknown.
items.tweetB: Dealers are short gamma AND CTAs have flipped short. The last time both lined up was the April unwind. Not predicting a repeat — but the pipeline for a non-linear move lower is assembled. CPI is the detonator.

charts.desc: CTA positioning vs US equities showing recent flip to net short

items.title: SocGen: 'Ingredients for a big top are still missing' — zero of three 1999 conditions are present today
items.idea: SocGen's Manish Kabra maps three conditions that preceded the 1999-2000 dot-com capex peak: (a) renewed Fed hiking cycle, (b) credit spreads rising 6-9 months before the peak, and (c) ISM firmly in expansion at 60. None of those three are present today. Kabra's conclusion: the ingredients for a structural top are still missing.
items.angle: Most useful as a monthly checklist. File this and revisit. The day one of those three conditions flips — especially credit spreads — is when the framework becomes actionable for de-risking.
items.tweetA: SocGen's framework for the 1999 top: Fed hiking, credit spreads rising, ISM at 60. None of those are true today. You can be worried about valuations AND still acknowledge the conditions for a structural peak haven't arrived.
items.tweetB: Three ingredients were present before the dot-com peak: Fed hiking, credit widening, ISM at 60. We have zero of three today. Either this cycle is different — or we're earlier in the cycle than bears think. SocGen says: not yet.

charts.desc: Top signals framework overlaying 1999 conditions vs 2026

items.source: Daily Chartbook / Ramp via Exponential View
items.title: AI-spending companies growing revenue 5x faster than economy; non-spenders tracking GDP
items.idea: Ramp data (via Exponential View) shows American companies spending the most on AI have seen their revenue grow five times faster than the economy as a whole. Non-spenders are tracking the economy closely. This bifurcation is early-stage evidence of AI-driven productivity divergence — a two-tier economy forming between adopters and laggards.
items.angle: This is the number that justifies the AI premium. The bear case on AI valuations has to explain why a 5x revenue growth differential won't be reflected in earnings over a 3-5 year horizon.
items.tweetA: Companies spending most on AI are growing revenue 5x faster than the broader economy. Non-spenders are just tracking GDP. The bifurcation is live. This isn't theoretical productivity gain — it's showing up in actual top-line data now.
items.tweetB: The AI valuation debate misses the simple point: there's already a measurable 5x revenue growth gap between AI-heavy spenders and laggards. That gap will compound. At some point 'too expensive' becomes 'I can't afford not to own it.'

charts.desc: Revenue growth: AI spenders vs non-spenders vs GDP growth

items.title: AI tape dead cat confirmed — market falling without a catalyst means positioning IS the catalyst
items.idea: Tuesday's session: Nasdaq -1.1%, S&P -0.26%, PHLX Semi -1.9%. The key tell: the market fell without a clear macro catalyst. Meanwhile, existing home sales +3.2% vs +0.7% estimate was treated as a rates problem, not a growth positive — the classic regime flip where good news is bad.
items.angle: This is the transition moment. Only a CPI soft enough to reopen the cut narrative changes the frame. The 'oil down = risk on' reflex and the 'good macro = risk on' reflex are both broken.
items.tweetA: Existing home sales beat by 4.5x the estimate on Tuesday. Stocks still fell. Good macro is now a rates problem, not a growth catalyst. The market has flipped the sign on good news. That's a regime change in how you should think about data.
items.tweetB: The market fell Tuesday without a macro catalyst. That's the tell. When positioning itself becomes the selling pressure — and AI leadership is crowded enough that it doesn't need news to crack — the unwind is self-reinforcing until it finds a real buyer.

items.title: SpaceX IPO Thursday: Morningstar fair value $63 = 53% discount to IPO price; moonshot only 7% probability; 4x oversubscribed
items.idea: Morningstar puts SpaceX fair value at $63/share — a 53% discount to the expected IPO price. Moonshot scenario ($154/share) receives only 7% probability. Yet demand is ~4x oversubscribed. If institutions fund SpaceX by trimming existing winners, it becomes a mechanical liquidity drain on AI longs exactly when they're already under pressure.
items.angle: A $1.75T IPO 4x oversubscribed but with a 53% discount from Morningstar is a stress test for the market's capacity to absorb AI equity supply. Watch where the cash comes from, not where it goes.
items.tweetA: Morningstar's SpaceX fair value: $63. Expected IPO price: ~$135. That's a 53% premium you're asked to pay on day one. 4x oversubscribed anyway. Either Morningstar is wrong, or 4x oversubscribed means institutional money is recycling from existing winners.
items.tweetB: SpaceX IPO Thursday. 4x oversubscribed, $1.75T ask. Morningstar base case = 53% downside. 7% chance of being worth more than the IPO price. The question isn't whether to buy SpaceX. It's what gets sold to fund it.

items.title: Tech sector dispersion hits 120pp — widest since February 2000, two months before the dot-com peak
items.idea: The spread between the best- and worst-performing quintiles in the tech sector now exceeds 120 percentage points, the widest since February 2000. Today's bottom quintile is down ~8% (vs up ~10% in 1999), meaning exuberance is narrower and more surgical. Narrow excess inside the most important sector in the index can still cause structural damage when it unwinds.
items.angle: Use the Feb 2000 parallel as a risk framework, not a prediction. The key watch: does this dispersion narrow via losers catching up, or via winners catching down? The mode of narrowing matters enormously.
items.tweetA: Tech sector dispersion just hit 120 percentage points between winners and losers — the widest since February 2000, two months before the Nasdaq peak. In 1999, even the bottom quintile was up. Today it's down 8%. Narrower exuberance doesn't make it safer.
items.tweetB: The dot-com parallel people keep dismissing: tech dispersion at levels not seen since Feb 2000. Today's losers are actually losing. Narrow AI exuberance inside an otherwise struggling sector is historically fragile.

items.title: Hyperscaler capex approaching ~100% of operating cash flow by year-end, up from 40% in 2023
items.idea: Hyperscaler capex as share of operating cash flow projected to reach ~100% by end of 2026, up from 40% in 2023. The AI buildout is consuming essentially all shareholder returns — buybacks slowed, cash flow conversion flat, both IG and equity supply increased. The AI machine is transitioning from asset-light software story to capital-intensive furnace.
items.angle: When capex = 100% of OCF, there is no free cash flow for buybacks or dividends. You're paying growth multiples for infrastructure companies. The valuation framework needs to change to reflect this.
items.tweetA: Hyperscaler capex as % of operating cash flow: 40% in 2023, heading to ~100% by year-end. The AI trade is now a capital-intensive infrastructure bet, not a software margin story. These are different animals. Price them accordingly.
items.tweetB: Three years ago hyperscalers spent 40 cents of every operating dollar on capex. By year-end it'll be nearly 100 cents. Buybacks slowing, cash flow conversion flat. The AI bull thesis is intact — but it now requires an infrastructure investor's lens.

items.title: Korea: investors 50+ account for 60%+ of $18.5B margin debt — leveraged AI mania crosses generations
items.idea: South Korea's margin loan balance at top 10 brokerages hit 27.2 trillion won (~$18.5B) in Q1 2026, with investors 50+ accounting for 60%+ — more than double last year. This generation is traditionally fixed-deposit and real estate holders. KORU (3x EWY) needs to double to return to levels from days ago. Upside volatility panic (buying calls into an 8% rally) was last seen in dot-com's final innings. ELS products embed short downside optionality forcing dealer hedging into sell-offs.
items.angle: Household balance sheet risk (retirement savings) entering a reflexive volatility ecosystem. Korea is the purest global expression of AI leverage risk — and spillover risk is no longer theoretical.
items.tweetA: Korean investors 50+ now hold 60% of $18.5B in margin debt at Korea's top brokerages — more than double last year. The fixed-deposit generation is now leveraged into AI chip ETFs. When leverage works the other way, it's not just a portfolio problem. It's a retirement problem.
items.tweetB: KORU (3x EWY) needs to double to get back to levels from days ago. Upside volatility panic in Korea — buying calls into an 8% rally — was last seen in the final innings of dot-com. The KOSPI isn't a market. It's 50+ year old household balance sheets strapped to a semiconductor roller coaster.

items.title: Treasury issuing $500B+ in T-bills per week — harmless until money market rates jump from oversupply
items.idea: US Treasury issuing $500B+ in T-bills per week, currently absorbed by ~$8T in money market funds. Administration is leaning on the short end because inflation above 2% for five years has lifted the term premium, making long-term borrowing more expensive. Risk: if supply overwhelms demand and money market rates jump, the 'harmless' bill strategy becomes a pressure valve in an already hot macro system.
items.angle: Watch for any stress in repo or money market rates — that's the signal the supply is starting to bite. The fiscal math is being managed through the T-bill market.
items.tweetA: $500B in T-bills per week. $8T in money market funds to absorb it. So far so good. But the reason we're here is that inflation above 2% for 5 years has made long-term borrowing too expensive. The bill market is the overflow valve. The question is when it starts to leak.
items.tweetB: The Treasury is borrowing $500B/week in T-bills to avoid the term premium on longer maturities. Works fine until money market rates jump from oversupply. Five years of inflation has pushed fiscal strategy to the shortest end of the curve. That's not a plan. It's a bet on the repo market.

items.title: WTI falls below $90 but lower oil produces no equity relief — the equity problem is positioning, not oil
items.idea: WTI fell below $90 after Trump suggested Iran deal 'in final throes,' then jumped when Trump threatened retaliation after Iran targeted a US Apache helicopter. WTI closed down >3%. More importantly: lower oil did not produce a clean rally in stocks or bonds. When crude falls, yields ease, dollar behaves, and equities still can't breathe — the equity problem is structural, not geopolitical.
items.angle: The 'oil down = risk on' reflex is broken in this environment. The equity weakness is not an oil story. That diagnosis matters for positioning.
items.tweetA: Oil fell 3%+ Tuesday, yields eased, dollar was calm. Stocks still couldn't rally. When all the macro relief factors align and equities still decline, the problem isn't macro. It's positioning. The AI unwind is doing damage that lower oil can't fix.
items.tweetB: Iran negotiations 'in final throes' → WTI drops. US Apache shot down → WTI spikes. WTI closes -3% anyway. Equities didn't respond to the oil relief at all. The market is telling you: the equity problem isn't about Iran or oil. It's about how crowded and expensive the leadership has gotten.

items.title: $70B ICE/CBP funded through Trump's entire term — immigration enforcement exits the annual budget hostage cycle
items.idea: House passed 214-212 (Senate 52-47) a $70B immigration enforcement bill for ICE and Border Patrol, funded through rest of Trump's term. Converts immigration enforcement from a recurring budget fight into a 3-year operating platform. Courts become the new chokepoint: more raids → more detention challenges → backlog-pressure loop.
items.angle: Private prison operators ($CXW, $GEO), detention contractors, transport/logistics firms serving federal enforcement now have multi-year revenue visibility. Political risk replaced by execution risk.
items.tweetA: $70B for ICE and CBP, funded through Trump's entire term — outside the annual appropriations cycle. Immigration enforcement just became a 3-year operating platform instead of a September hostage negotiation. The bottleneck has shifted from Congress to DHS execution capacity.
items.tweetB: The immigration bill passed 214-212 — two votes. But the structural change is profound: ICE is funded through 2029 outside normal budget politics. Courts become the next chokepoint: more raids = more detention challenges = backlog loop. $CXW $GEO are the unobvious read-through.

items.title: All three mega-AI IPOs confidentially filed: SpaceX ($1.75T), OpenAI, Anthropic — trillion-dollar trio
items.idea: SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic all confidentially filed within days of each other. SpaceX prices Friday ($1.75T). OpenAI timing is fall 2026. Anthropic is third. Parekh's ranking: Anthropic and OpenAI best risk/reward (enterprise/coding PMF); SpaceX/xAI has least attractive risk/reward. OpenAI has ChatGPT as fastest-growing consumer software product to date.
items.angle: Three concurrent trillion-dollar AI IPOs is an equity supply event unlike anything seen before. The question is whether the market has capacity to absorb all three without forcing major rotation out of existing AI positions.
items.tweetA: SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic — all confidentially filed, all above $1T — in the same month. I've been in markets long enough to remember when three simultaneous IPOs above $50B was a big deal. We are in a genuinely different era.
items.tweetB: Three AI IPOs above $1T asking simultaneously for public market capital. Parekh's ranking: Anthropic and OpenAI best risk/reward, SpaceX/xAI least attractive. The market will have to vote with actual dollars very soon.

items.title: SpaceX bakes in $60B Cursor acquisition — largest private tech purchase ever, $10B walkaway fee if IPO fails
items.idea: SpaceX baked a $60B acquisition of Cursor into the IPO structure — largest private tech purchase on record. Contingent on SpaceX IPO succeeding; $10B walkaway fee if it fails. Cursor gets excess compute from Colossus — same infrastructure rented to Anthropic ($40B) and Google ($30B). Creates AI coding infrastructure network anchored to single platform.
items.angle: The $10B walkaway fee alone would rank as one of the largest tech acquisitions in history. The contingency structure is an option that becomes a deal. Watch Cursor's implied valuation if SpaceX prices below expectations.
items.tweetA: $60B for Cursor — the AI coding tool. Contingent on SpaceX IPO. If the IPO fails, $10B walkaway fee. The walkaway fee alone would be one of the largest tech acquisitions ever. Elon has structured the SpaceX IPO so that declining it costs $10 billion.
items.tweetB: Elon is building SpaceX/xAI as an AI infrastructure platform: rent Colossus to Anthropic ($40B), rent it to Google ($30B), acquire Cursor for $60B. Every deal feeds the ecosystem. The IPO is the mechanism that locks in the flywheel.

items.title: Apple iOS 27 gates most powerful AI to 12GB+ RAM — memory prices up 10-30%, Nvidia and Apple have locked supply
items.idea: iOS 27's most powerful on-device AI features require 12GB+ RAM, gating to iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone Air only. Base iPhone 17: 8GB. SK Hynix/Micron/Samsung retasked capacity to data centers — consumer memory prices up 10-30%. Nvidia and Apple locked up supply at best prices for 2-3 years. Same RAM constraint hits Windows and Android.
items.angle: Hardware upgrade supercycle disguised as software announcement. Base-tier hardware cannot run the best AI features — forcing premium upgrades. Good for Apple ASPs, good for memory pricing. Two demand forces (cloud + device) hitting simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Apple just gated its most powerful AI behind 12GB of RAM. Base iPhone 17: 8GB. Your current Mac probably doesn't qualify either. This isn't a software launch. It's a hardware upgrade cycle in disguise. SK Hynix and Micron are supply-locked by Apple and Nvidia for 2-3 years.
items.tweetB: Memory prices up 10-30% because SK Hynix/Micron retasked capacity to data centers. Now Apple requires 12GB+ for its best AI features. Two forces driving memory demand simultaneously: cloud AI training AND consumer AI inference on device. The supply math doesn't work unless prices keep rising.

items.source: Bankless / William Peaster + CFTC Chairman Mike Selig
items.title: CFTC approves first onshore US Bitcoin perpetual on Kalshi — perps are 90% of all crypto trading volume
items.idea: CFTC greenlit first onshore US Bitcoin perpetual (on Kalshi) plus no-action letter clearing Coinbase to route to Deribit. Perps = ~90% of all crypto trading volume. Within one week, 17 assets beyond Bitcoin were self-certified or in process. Digital commodities can now self-certify listing without pre-approval. Coinbase, Kalshi, Kraken, Robinhood, and Hyperliquid all angling for US market simultaneously.
items.angle: Onshoring 90% of crypto trading volume is a structural liquidity event for US-regulated venues. The revenue implications for $COIN are significant and not yet fully priced.
items.tweetA: The CFTC just onshored the instrument that makes up 90% of all crypto trading volume. First US Bitcoin perp live on Kalshi. 17 more assets self-certified within a week. The offshore crypto trading era — born from regulatory failure — is officially ending.
items.tweetB: Coinbase can now route customers to Deribit. Kalshi has the first onshore BTC perp. Kraken, Robinhood all moving. The CFTC just handed US exchanges the key to the most-traded instrument in crypto. The revenue implications for $COIN are not fully priced.

items.title: US perps capped at 5-7x leverage vs 250x offshore — Selig: 'I do not want another FTX'
items.idea: CFTC Chairman Selig confirmed US perps will have: registered exchanges, clearinghouses ringfencing customer funds, and leverage caps at 5-7x (maximum 10x). Offshore: 250x. Selig noted the CFTC-regulated FTX entity was the one that kept customer funds safe during FTX's collapse. Hyperliquid almost certainly won't bolt on KYC.
items.angle: Two parallel ecosystems forming: onshore for regulated institutional capital (5-7x), offshore for degens (250x). Hyperliquid's refusal to add KYC may end up being its competitive moat with the leverage-seeking crowd.
items.tweetA: US perps: 5-7x leverage max. Offshore perps: 250x. Selig's line was clear: 'I do not want another FTX.' He's building a domesticated product — guardrails, clearinghouses, ringfenced funds. It's not for degens. It's for regulated capital. That's actually a bigger market in dollar terms.
items.tweetB: The CFTC is opening onshore perps for institutional capital. The degens will stay on Hyperliquid. Two parallel crypto derivatives ecosystems are forming — one for compliance, one for leverage. Hyperliquid's refusal to KYC might end up being its competitive moat.

items.title: RWA perps (oil, gold, pre-IPO OpenAI/Anthropic) dominate offshore volume — onshoring requires SEC-CFTC cooperation
items.idea: RWA perps — oil, gold, equity indices, pre-IPO names like OpenAI and Anthropic — already make up majority of perps volume, almost entirely offshore. Equity perps require CFTC and SEC to cooperate (perp = CFTC domain; stock = SEC domain). Selig acknowledged the challenge. If it happens, creates onshore market for leveraged exposure to pre-IPO AI companies.
items.angle: 12-18 months away at minimum. But if US investors can trade a leveraged perp on OpenAI or Anthropic before IPO, it changes liquidity dynamics for those names pre-listing. Watch this space in 2027.
items.tweetA: The next frontier in perps onshoring: oil, gold, and pre-IPO names like OpenAI and Anthropic. Already actively traded offshore. Bringing them onshore requires the CFTC and SEC to cooperate on a single product. These two agencies haven't cooperated well in years.
items.tweetB: Imagine trading a leveraged perp on OpenAI or Anthropic before they IPO — legally, in the US. That's what RWA perps onshoring eventually enables. The regulatory coordination is the hard part. But demand is obvious. Watch this space in 2027.

items.title: Morpho raises $175M (Paradigm, a16z, Ribbit Capital) — DeFi lending protocol hits $11B deposits
items.idea: Morpho raised $175M co-led by Paradigm, a16z crypto, and Ribbit Capital to build its 'open credit network.' $11B in deposits. Ribbit Capital's involvement is the structural signal — Ribbit normally backs TradFi-adjacent companies (Robinhood, Nubank, Credit Karma), not DeFi protocols.
items.angle: Ribbit is the tell. Their presence signals institutional deployment path is now credible. Morpho at $11B deposits with this trio is the blueprint for DeFi credit infrastructure at scale.
items.tweetA: Morpho just raised $175M from Paradigm, a16z, and Ribbit Capital. $11B in deposits. Ribbit normally backs Robinhood and Nubank, not DeFi protocols. When TradFi credit investors start leading DeFi rounds, something structural has shifted.
items.tweetB: DeFi lending hitting $11B deposits (Morpho). $175M raise from a16z + Paradigm + Ribbit. The narrative that DeFi credit is niche for crypto-natives is hard to sustain when Ribbit Capital — backer of Robinhood, Nubank, Credit Karma — is co-leading the round.

items.title: Blackstone caps withdrawals on private credit funds — first redemption gate of the private credit cycle
items.idea: Blackstone has capped withdrawals on private credit funds amid credit turmoil — echoing BREIT redemption gates in 2022-23. Cliffwater also mentioned. When the largest alternative asset manager restricts liquidity in a credit product, it signals elevated redemption pressure or underlying portfolio stress making NAV marks unreliable. Private credit has been the hottest institutional allocation theme for 3 years.
items.angle: Watch for similar moves at Ares, Blue Owl, and other large direct lending platforms. The 'private credit is the new high yield' trade is facing its first real liquidity test. The 2022 BREIT analogy lasted 18+ months.
items.tweetA: Blackstone just gated its private credit funds. We saw this movie with BREIT in 2022 — it wasn't pretty. Private credit has been the institutional allocation trade for 3 years. The first redemption gate is not the end. It's the beginning of a conversation about what these things are actually worth.
items.tweetB: Private credit gates at Blackstone and Cliffwater simultaneously. The 'private credit is the new high yield' trade is facing its first real liquidity stress test. When the two largest platforms gate at the same time — that's not noise. That's a regime signal.

items.title: Tech selloff is mechanical not fundamental — 3 giant IPOs mean T+1 cash must be raised by EOD Wednesday
items.idea: Phil Dauber's read: much of Tuesday's tech selloff is mechanical, not fundamental. Three massive IPOs arriving this week (SpaceX Thursday). T+1 settlement means institutions need cash by EOD Wednesday — they're selling winners to fund new positions. 'Look for some of this sell pressure to abate' once the settlement window closes. Tape 'feels heavy' but the mechanical driver is time-bounded.
items.angle: Most actionable near-term read. If the IPO cash-raise thesis is correct, the selling in tech today and Wednesday is NOT a structural break — it's a window to add. Risk: if SpaceX prices badly, mechanical support disappears AND structural fragility gets tested simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Phil Dauber's read on Tuesday's tech selloff: it's mostly mechanical. SpaceX IPO is Thursday. T+1 settlement means institutions need cash by EOD Wednesday. They're selling winners to fund the new position. Not a breakdown — a time-bounded drain. Watch the tape Thursday morning.
items.tweetB: 3 mega-IPOs in one week + T+1 settlement = tech gets sold Wednesday to fund Thursday allocation. Dauber: 'look for sell pressure to abate.' I'd add: unless SpaceX opens badly — at which point the mechanical bid disappears AND the structural fragility gets tested simultaneously.
